title: Nuclear Fission and Fusion
focus: Comparison of fundamental processes, fuel requirements, energy output, and waste generation between nuclear fission and fusion.
rationale: Basic nuclear physics concepts are frequently tested in UPSC Prelims, particularly the differences in energy yield and radioactive waste.
sectionExcerpt: Fission occurs when a neutron slams into a larger atom, forcing it to excite and split into two smaller atoms... Fusion occurs when two atoms slam together to form a heavier atom... Fusion could generate four times more energy per kilogram of fuel than fission... Fusion on the other hand does not create any long-lived radioactive nuclear waste. Only helium and neutrons.
evidenceBullets: Fission uses fuels like Uranium and Plutonium, while fusion generally uses hydrogen isotopes Deuterium (D) and Tritium (T).
Fusion can generate four times more energy per kilogram of fuel compared to fission.
Fission produces long-lived radioactive waste (e.g., iodine, caesium, strontium), whereas fusion only produces helium and neutrons without long-lived radioactive waste.
Fusion requires extreme conditions, such as plasma reaching 150,000,000 °C in a tokamak.
Net Energy Gain (NEG) in commercial fusion implies the process generates more energy than it consumes.

title: Tokamak Reactors and ITER Project
focus: Working principles of Tokamak reactors, the international ITER project, and India's contributions and domestic tokamak research.
rationale: ITER is a landmark global scientific initiative with significant Indian participation. Knowledge of global and Indian tokamak reactors is highly relevant for Science & Tech questions.
sectionExcerpt: ITER has completed its central magnet system, with India playing a critical role... India has manufactured cryostat, an enormous chamber that houses the entire ITER Tokamak... Tokamak is designed to harness the energy of fusion. In it, fusion plasma is created and confined by strong magnetic fields.
evidenceBullets: ITER is located in France and aims to demonstrate fusion as an abundant, safe, carbon-free energy source.
India manufactured the cryostat (chamber housing the tokamak) and cryolines (carrying liquid helium to cool magnets) for ITER.
The Institute for Plasma Research in Ahmedabad is the lead Indian institution participating in ITER.
A Tokamak reactor works by creating and confining fusion plasma using strong magnetic fields.
Major global tokamaks include JET (UK), EAST (China), KSTAR (South Korea), and JT-60SA (Japan/Europe).
India's domestic tokamak research includes ADITYA (first indigenously designed), ADITYA-U, and SST-1.

title: Fast Breeder Reactor (FBR)
focus: India's Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor (PFBR), its fuel and coolant mechanisms, and its role in Stage II of India's nuclear program.
rationale: India's three-stage nuclear program is a core UPSC topic. The commissioning of the PFBR and its technical specifics (MOX fuel, liquid sodium) are highly examinable.
sectionExcerpt: India’s first Fast-Breeder Nuclear Reactor (500 MWe) is set for commissioning by 2026... Uses indigenously produced Uranium-Plutonium Mixed Oxide (MOX) fuel and utilize liquid sodium as a coolant. Uranium-238 blanket surrounding fuel core will undergo nuclear transmutation to produce more fuel, thus earning the name ‘Breeder’.
evidenceBullets: BHAVINI was established by the government to construct and operate the Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor (PFBR) at Kalpakkam, Tamil Nadu.
The PFBR uses Uranium-Plutonium Mixed Oxide (MOX) fuel and liquid sodium as a coolant.
It is called a 'Breeder' because a Uranium-238 blanket undergoes nuclear transmutation to produce more fuel than it consumes.
India will be the second country after Russia to have a commercial operating Fast Breeder Reactor.
The operationalization of the PFBR marks the beginning of Stage II of India’s three-stage nuclear power program.
In the FBR, Thorium will undergo transmutation to create fissile U-233, which will serve as fuel for the third stage of the nuclear program.
